corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: The u-net (averaged over 7 rotated versions of the input data) achieves without any further pre-or postprocessing a warping error of 0.0003529 (the new best score, see Table ) and a rand-error of 0.0382.
The network architecture is illustrated in Figure .
The contracting path follows the typical architecture of a convolutional network.
In this paper, we present a network and training strategy that relies on the strong use of data augmentation to use the available annotated samples more efficiently.
The architecture consists of a contracting path to capture context and a symmetric expanding path that enables precise localization.

We show that such a network can be trained end-to-end from very few images and outperforms the prior best method (a sliding-window convolutional network) on the ISBI challenge for segmentation of neuronal structures in electron microscopic stacks.

We show that such a network can be trained end-to-end from very few images and outperforms the prior best method (a sliding-window convolutional network) on the ISBI challenge for segmentation of neuronal structures in electron microscopic stacks.
Using the same network trained on transmitted light microscopy images (phase contrast and DIC) we won the ISBI cell tracking challenge 2015 in these categories by a large margin.
Using the same network trained on transmitted light microscopy images (phase contrast and DIC) we won the ISBI cell tracking challenge 2015 in these categories by a large margin.
In the last two years, deep convolutional networks have outperformed the state of the art in many visual recognition tasks, e.g. .
The breakthrough by Krizhevsky et al. was due to supervised training of a large network with 8 layers and millions of parameters on the ImageNet dataset with 1 million training images.
The resulting network won the EM segmentation challenge at ISBI 2012 by a large margin.
Secondly, there is a trade-off between localization accuracy and the use of context.
Larger patches require more max-pooling layers that reduce the localization accuracy, while small patches allow the network to see only little context.
In this paper, we build upon a more elegant architecture, the so-called "fully convolutional network" .
We modify and extend this architecture such that it works with very few training images and yields more precise segmentations; see Figure .

One important modification in our architecture is that in the upsampling part we have also a large number of feature channels, which allow the network to propagate context information to higher resolution layers.
As a consequence, the expansive path is more or less symmetric to the contracting path, and yields a u-shaped architecture.
This allows the network to learn invariance to such deformations, without the need to see these transformations in the annotated image corpus.
Another challenge in many cell segmentation tasks is the separation of touching objects of the same class; see Figure .
To this end, we propose the use of a weighted loss, where the separating background labels between touching cells obtain a large weight in the loss function.
Furthermore, we show results for cell segmentation in light microscopy images from the ISBI cell tracking challenge 2015.
The data set is provided by the EM segmentation challenge that was started at ISBI 2012 and is still open for new contributions.
This segmenation task is part of the ISBI cell tracking challenge 2014 and 2015 .
Here we achieve an average IOU ("intersection over union") of 92%, which is significantly better than the second best algorithm with 83% (see Table ).
